Okay, so, you know, Turnabout is fair play. I've introduced you to seven great copywriters, all the different points in their career, all had different stories about how they got started. I figured it was only fair to give you a little bit of my background and tell you what I went through getting into this business. Guillermo. Well, let me back up a little bit. I was born a poor black child. That's when a Carleen's favorite lies. My mother was the life of every party, evidently, had nine children with eight different guys, never got married, and had a very tragic death at a young age. I was adopted by a Methodist minister who had brain damage, and his wonderful wife, he had been in a car wreck when he was younger and nobody knew it was brain damage, they just thought he was weird, you know? And so, I was raised by them, my mom was just a saint, my dad was just kind of out there, and he became a Methodist minister, and he was not a very good one. And so, he always got the little tiny churches, you know, 30 people in the congregation was a big deal, you know? And sometimes had to take three churches at a time, and he preached the same sermon three times every morning at three different churches in little tiny towns in Minnesota. I don't think he ever earned more than $600 a month in his whole life. And he, we were so poor, you know, that if we didn't have a personage given to us by the church, we would have been homeless, you know? And the church also supplied us with cars. We got a car, a house, and then this tiny salary. So all the time I was growing up, it was like, you know how kids always say we didn't know we were poor? We knew we were poor. It was just bologna, we knew it, you know? And so, you know, we were raised in that way. At the beginning of every school year, I'd get a pair of blue jeans and two shirts. And other than what the people in the church would bring us to give to us, you know, the hand-me-downs during the year, that was it. So by the end of the year, I had holes in all my clothes and I was, you know, two sizes too big for them, you know? So that was growing up. And I remember always wanting something better and always being fantasizing, always fantasizing about the people around me that were doing so much better. And I remember later on in life when I got a car, I would drive around, I lived in Tulsa at the time, I would drive around town and I would look at the houses. It was the second richest city per capita in the country at the time, and because of the pipeline industry. And so, I went around town, I'd look at the mansions and realize that I didn't have enough money to buy a single brick in their driveway, you know? And look at how these people are living. It fascinated me. So I'm in high school. My mom and dad split up and I had to quit school to go to work to support my mom and my younger brother, which I did happily because I was really bored to tears in school anyway. And so, I had three jobs and one of them happened to be in a print shop, but I was working at a gas station, a donut shop, and then eventually a print shop. And in that print shop, I got to run the folding machine for doing direct mailings. And so, I spent my nights just reading these direct mail pieces. And they happened to be fundraising pieces. They were, you know, this organization was like for God and country kind of stuff and very patriotic, very political, and very fundamentally Christian, right? So I would read these things and I got it immediately. There was just something about these letters that was easy to understand. You know, we all talk about how hard it is to sell a product, okay? Imagine that you're a copywriter and your job is to talk somebody into just giving you money, right? What's the benefit? Hey, give me 10 bucks, you'll be 10 bucks poorer. You know? There is no benefit to that. It's not going to make you taller, skinnier, richer, you know, healthier, or anything else. So it's a real challenge to write fundraising copy, but I was fascinated with it for that very reason. And the reason that I am so into emote, dominant emotion advertising today is because they, that's all they had to work with. Those letters, that's all they had to work with. They had to either scare the bejesus out of you or get you feeling sorry about a poor little dog that was going to be abused or had been abused. It was all emotion. And what I was buying from my $10 contribution was this catharsis, this feeling good that I'd done something good. And when that's all you have to sell, it is a challenge. And I was fascinated with how these people wrote. I became especially fascinated when I found out the guy who wrote these letters, his name was Steve Winchell, in 1968 was making $650,000 a year. You know, that's big bucks. So you know, I got really interested in it and I went to the head of the organization that was writing all these letters and I said, I'd like to try writing one of these. And I'm like 19. And he goes, oh, okay, that'd be fun. Why don't you do it? And I said, well, you mail it if I do. And he said, well, if it's good enough, we'll mail it. So I wrote this four-page letter for this organization and I used just, it was instinctive. It was like, I'm going to use the same kind of emotional tools that I've just been learning, sitting there all night long reading these appeal letters, you know, at the folding machine. And I wrote the package and they mailed it against one of Steve Winchell's package, the $650,000 guy. And I won. So being a complete idiot, it never occurred to me that this was the way I could make money. So I kept running the folding machine and, you know, cooking donuts and working in the gas station and wondering why I couldn't afford a brick in that driveway. And so I was offered a job in that organization, had a media department, television department. So I was offered a job in that television department. And I took it. They needed a gopher, basically, a guy who'd run around and, you know, take film from here to there or whatever. And I got into the job and I did what I've been talking to you about, you know. You can look at a job as just how I have to spend eight hours a day, you know, five days a week before I can get to a weekend. Or you can look at it as an opportunity. And I looked at it in that way. I didn't know anything about television production. I didn't know about, you know, how to operate a camera. Editing back then was a trip. Any of you who are into television at all or understand the process. Back today, you have a laptop and you simply, you know, do a couple of things on this laptop and it'll do cuts and dissolves and whatever the heck you want to do. Back then you had two-inch videotape like this and you had three machines, right? A roll, B roll, and then the recording roll here. And you had to time everything and get the thing rolling at the right time and then hit recorded exactly the right moment. And if you screwed up, then you had to reset everything up. And it was just impossible. But it was challenging and it was fun. So I wanted to learn that stuff. Within a year they had fired my boss. And we were on 350 stations around the country. Within a year they had fired my boss. And at like 19 or 20, I was their choice to run this department, right? So they sent me out to Columbia Film School in Hollywood where I spent a few weeks studying under some of the, some really good directors and stuff. And I added other skills to my stable. Not only could I write and direct now, I could also operate a film camera. I could operate a video camera. I was pretty good with an aggro recorder for doing double system sound and film, you know, that kind of thing. And I could set up a remote truck and do all that kind of stuff. So I was doing that and I loved it. It was fun. It was exciting. I was meeting people like Jaeger Gabor and Ringo Starr and Walter Brennan was a favorite. He was just really a cool guy. So I did that for a while. And all of a sudden, now we're looking at 73, 74. And I had my own studio in Hollywood at the old Columbia Studios. I had my own three-camera truck. And I was, during the week, I was going out and selling facilities and doing commercials for people and stuff like that. And on weekends, I was taping church services. And so, you know, it was an okay living. But in 73, 74, we had a killer recession in this country. That's where we had that big gas scare. And this is in LA now, okay? So you go to gas station because you have an odd numbered license plate. You go on Tuesdays. And so you go and they run out of gas before you fill up. And so you park your car in line and walk home. And that's how bad it was. And then, so all of a sudden, there is no video, freelance video work to be done anywhere. And so, you know, what am I going to do? How am I going to feed my family? So I saw an ad in the paper. My wife actually saw it. Angelica saw this ad in the paper for a copywriter. And I said, you know, I did that once. I'm not kidding. It's like four or five years later. I said, I did that once. I know how to do that. It worked out okay. So maybe I should audition for this job, try out this job. So I went down and I had a meeting with a guy. And the guy says, oh, we want you to audition. We want you to write an appeal letter for us. You know? And he could have said, I want you to sell investment grade diamonds or stocks or, you know, anything. And, but no, it was an appeal letter. He wanted me to write. So I go, okay. I said, for who? And he goes, make up an organization. I get to make up an organization. That means I get to pick the malady that this organization is going to fix. That means I can pick the most emotionally wrenching. It's got to be a child. It's got to be something to do with a child. So, so I made it all up. I wrote this appeal letter. The guy looked like he was going to cry by the time he finished reading. He offered me a job on the spot. So this is a guy that ended up screwing me a few years later. I told you about yesterday. But anyway, I was in the business now. And the first thing that happened to me was that they gave me the books that we've been giving to you guys, the masters. And the admonition was, don't even come to work for two weeks. Read these books. Okay. And I did. And so the next thing he did was he gave me all of these books on economics. Theory of Money and Credit by Ludwig von Meises, which is Austrian economics. Stuffed by Milton Friedman. Stuffed by Adam Smith. Okay. So I got a good basic founding in economics, which was the background for investment. I then got some other books on investment. So this is all self-taught. There were no, there wasn't AWI then. There were no courses on how to write copy. There was a small, very clickish group of people who had discovered that you can slap black ink on white paper and make money. You can do that and people will send money to you. The feeling that Paris had when he went to his mailbox and saw all those envelopes there. And there was such a fraternity there. When you hear about David working with Ogilvy, who his first love, and in the book he says, my first love is direct mail. And then working for Rosenthal. And Rosenthal and John Finn were friends. It was this little fraternity of people who were like insiders. They got it. They had this secret, this way that you can turn nothing into something, this way that you can make money with just copywriting. Just by saying stuff on paper. So I started writing copy every single day for these guys. And they had no creating process. I mean, I was like the second employee. And there was no creating process. Until we got to this one opportunity that came along, Jarvis and Gann had an initiative to lower property taxes in California, Proposition 13. And they sent out an open invitation for anybody that wanted to try. They would mail your package to write a fundraising package to raise money to get Proposition 13 passed. And so my boss heard about it, told me about it. So I went out and I wrote a promotion to raise money for Proposition 13 for lowering taxes. Now since I came from fundraising, my first instinct was to get my car, go down to the beach where these houses were built for $5,000 20 years ago. But now they were so high in price that people couldn't even afford to pay the property tax on them. And start talking to some of these people and what the house meant to them. It was their father's house. It was their grandfather's house. What it would mean if they had to leave. And they were about ready to leave. And I was able to just give these stories, story after story after story of the catastrophe that would happen to these very real people because of these clutching evil government boobs. So you can tell I got into it. I really got into this. I wrote it. And of all the ad agencies in LA, all the copywriters in LA, we won. So the company got royalties on that. So that was another kind of confirmation for me that I could kind of do this. But in the meantime, everything I was doing for my other clients was just bombing. Absolutely dying. So it was really hard for me. I think it's easier if you're working for an agency, you've been hired by somebody to have failures and deal with it. There are other people in this with you. But when you're a freelancer and you're hired with such high expectations and you go in and you have a bomb, then it's harder to deal with. Which is one of the reasons. I understand the desire, the need for freedom and to do this on your own in your own time. But all of us here, all of the experts that you've met this weekend would probably tell you that spending a year at a company where you have to write copy every day, where you're copy chief by somebody is not a bad way to get going. I respect the way you're doing it. I'm a rebel. I've always been a renegade. Well, look at me. I went see my doctor on Thursday. I make twice what he makes. I found out basically what he makes. He went to school for 12 years. I read a few books and I got into this. So you want to do it on your own? Cool. That's great. I admire that. But also, it's equally valid to do it the other way too and get some really fast track training. So anyway, that guy screwed me out of a $20,000 bonus so I stole all the clients that I had brought him and went out on my own and created copy overload and did all those things I was telling you about Schemask and the Loaded 45 and all that stuff. That was basically the beginning. Now, see, I didn't, I wasn't like Guillermo where I was following my fear. I was following the money. That was it. I couldn't work in my chosen field anymore so I got an ad in the LA Times to tell me to be a copywriter. Okay, so I'm going to be a copywriter now. So I went and I did the copywriting. It didn't work out with that agency because the guy was crooked and so, okay, I'll go do this on my own. And during those periods of time, now remember, I came from one pair of jeans and two shirts a year, right? And now I have a wife and a child. We're living in an apartment. I'll never forget this apartment. For some reason, someone thought it would be a good idea if on the wall where the couch goes that they put a speaker in the wall about that big with a big grill over it. Really weird. And we were close to the beach but I mean we were living poor. And when I had to go out on my own, it was a real challenge. We went through some hard times there. And there were a lot of times when Angelica wanted me to quit. She didn't want me to pursue this. She was into security. And why not? She's a mother. She has two children. She has to make sure that are taken care of. So why not, right? So there was some natural stress and tension there. I didn't have her support. There were a lot of times when I wanted to quit. Harris did his whole thing on credit cards. I couldn't get a credit card. The credit card companies just looked at me and basically said you got to be joking, right? So we were kiting checks. What that means is on Monday you write a check to buy dinner. And then on Tuesday you write a check to cover that check. And on Wednesday you cut a check to cover those two checks. And you have to have two bank accounts. And so, I mean, at one point we're riding down the street on my motorcycle, right? And I had a traffic ticket that I had forgotten to pay. And so in fact I was at a direct mail conference in Beverly Hills. And leaving the conference I got this daggum ticket. So cop pulls me over. There's a warrant out for your arrest because you haven't paid this ticket. So he puts handcuffs on me, throws me in the back of his car, leaves my motorcycle on the street, my wife standing next to it. We have no cash, no money of any kind. She then has to call a cab, go to supermarket number one, cash a check for 70 bucks. She has no money on her. Cash a check for 70 bucks. Go to supermarket number two, cash another check for 70 bucks because my bail was 100 bucks. And that was the maximum amount that you could get. And then I come back, pay my bail and pay the cab driver. And then we got back on the motorcycle and finished our trip. That's a ridiculous situation for somebody with two kids to be in. But it was a sacrifice that I made for something that I believed in, that I really thought we could make. You know, when Paul talks about living in Waynesville with his wife and kids in Detroit, it just breaks your heart. He's got a fire in his belly. And he's not going to let anything stand between him and the vision he has of where he's going to be in a few years with what he can now do, especially combining copywriting with product launch formula. So the rest of the story is fairly typical. I mean, we started writing on our own. We started writing, you heard the story about Phoenix. That was my next breakthrough where I called Johnny Johnson and told him his copy sucked. Again, courage, working through your fears, working through intimidation, just doing it. Let the chips fall where they may. You're going to write your own story someday. And there are going to be stories like this that you'll be telling people, okay? But it's following what makes sense to you. So then we're in Prescott. I'm making maybe 90,000 a year now. Things are looking up a little bit. And that's when I came into contact with Security Rare Coins. Now, this is the part of the story. The first part of the story is basically rags to riches, right? It's $600 a year. My dad made no money for anything, dead broke, right? And no hope of really getting out of it. The second part of the story, though, has to do with working smart. And it has to do with what we've been looking at today in terms of putting partnerships together and leveraging each client. Because that's when I got together with Security Rare Coin. Guy calls me up, will you write a package? I'll write a package, wrote the package. Asked him if I could write his back-end promotion. He said, yes. Bang, I'm in for a quarter million dollars a year plus 5% of sales. And we're doing $160 million a year in sales. So that's pretty substantial money, right? So then find out he's a crook. Quit. Now, when I tell this story, and I've told it several times or referred to it, I'm just encouraging you do not betray your own values. It's not worth it, OK? I don't care if it's not illegal. I don't care if it doesn't break a regulation. If you don't feel right about it, if you don't feel like the customer is getting the long end of this, OK? Then walk away. Just give it up, walk away. Stay away from that stuff. I think all of our mom has raised us better than that, right? So when I quit him, he said he was putting out a contract on my life. And he was going to have my wife's legs broken. He gave me the choice. He said, you can come back to work for me on Monday morning. And I will give you a check for $1 million. Or if you leave, I will put out a contract on you and have Angelica's legs broken. So we didn't even take time to pack. We jumped in the car. We drove to New Orleans. Talked to Jim Blanchard down there who had the second largest coin company. That was the genre, excuse me, the niche that we were in at the time. And structured even sweeter deal with him because not only did I get my quarter of a million, not only did I get my 5% of sales, but I got him to agree that if Bill sued me, he would pay all the legal bills. And so beautiful deal. We really enjoyed New Orleans, another big success there. I told you also the next step was getting together with those publishing. Because of the work I did at Blanchard, Bob King there was very impressed. And as soon as I left Blanchard, gave me a lot of assignments. So in 1995, I hit $1 million a year in income. And it was because of Gary Ben-Savanga. Gary was the first one I know of, Paris may correct me on this, but he was the first one I know of to demand a royalty. We had been working in the 70s and most of the 80s on straight fee. It's all England would pay. But we had this wonderful thing happening, which was computers coming up, which meant we had a lot less work. We didn't have to retype a whole draft every time we did an editing pass. We got fax machines. So we could transmit copy quicker and take more time to actually do the copy instead of shipping it all. I mean, I at one point had to buy an airplane seat for some art boards to send to a client in New York to make his deadline. So all these wonderful things are happening technologically to make my job easier. But then at the same time, Gary Ben-Savanga goes royalties. What a concept. Everyone else, if I write a movie, I get royalties. If I write a book, I get royalties. Why wouldn't a direct response copywriter get royalties? And so the minute I heard this, and this has happened several times in my life, I just feel like an idiot. Why is it Gary has to always come up with these things? It was a no brainer. Of course, I deserve a royalty. So I went to Phillips when I was talking to Bob. And I said, OK, guys, here's what I want from now on. Is that OK? And everyone's, oh, yeah, no problem. We'll pay your royalties. So at first it was $10,000. Then it was 20. Then it was 50. So in 95, we hit a million bucks a year. And I've never looked back. And it's just, it's rewarding now at this point in my life to look back at having, you know how it is, the old man talking about his accomplishments. And you look at those things, and it makes you feel good. In some senses, a life well-spent kind of thing. But the thing I'm proudest of, really, are the seven people in this room that you've met this week. We've got, you know, Paris is so sweet about giving me credit for some of his success. But the fact of the matter is, there's nothing could have stopped him. There is nothing that could have stopped that man. Same thing with Carline. She was showing really good copywriting abilities when she was still at Phillips. Nothing would have stopped her. Marcella is my granddaughter in the trade. The same thing with her. Try to stop her. Try to get a word in edgewise. Try to stop her. And these new guys I'm working with now are so cool. Paul with his passion, you know, and Guillermo with his genius. I mean, his approach to, when you see the promotion for this weekend, you know, the videos of this weekend, that's all G. That's all G. And he's just awesome, awesome. So the most satisfying thing for me has been helping others. So my admonition to you is to, if you haven't already tonight, get together. Form copywriting communes, call it whatever you will. Support each other, okay? Support each other. And there'll be a time in the not too distant future when people will be thanking you for getting their career started, thanking you for giving them the answer, thanking you for giving them the freedom. So that's it. Thank you.
